title: Telemarketing Bureaus and Other Contact Centers

narrative.executive_view: This is a poor lower-middle-market acquisition despite substantial task exposure. NAICS 561422 is specifically a third-party contact-center service, not a software provider or the client brand [S1]. AI can reduce routine-agent and QA work, but that same capability attacks the seat/hour economics the operator sells. A buyer should underwrite a melting base unless the target already has contracted outcome pricing, defensible regulated workflows, and evidence that its clients will not take the workflow in-house or move it to a platform.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: Routine inbound service, outbound qualification, reminders, collections prompts, summarization, knowledge retrieval, after-call work and interaction QA are technically exposed. The judgment is not that every conversation becomes autonomous: escalations, complaint handling, complex collections and client-specific exception work remain human-supervised. The supplied occupational mix is a 4-digit 5614 proxy and contains implausible sibling-code roles, so the role estimate is deliberately broad rather than falsely precise. Concentrix itself identifies AI, GenAI, RPA, ML, IVR and self-service as capable of replacing or supplementing human contacts [S2].
narrative.value_capture: Savings are unlikely to stay with a small bureau in full. The Census definition says these establishments act for clients and do not own the represented product or service [S1], which makes the client relationship contestable when a client can buy automation directly. Concentrix warns that automation can change pricing models, reduce the available market and create pricing pressure [S2]. Usage-priced AI contact-center technology is also being commercialized by platform vendors [S4]. A niche operator can retain some savings through compliance, integrations and execution, but per-agent or per-interaction contracts make pass-through and volume loss the central case.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective adoption should reach half of routine eligible work in roughly three years, not immediately. Existing IVR, self-service and assisted-agent deployments reduce the implementation burden, while security review, integration, client approval and error handling slow full workflow replacement. I set current effective adoption at 20%: that is an estimate of work already materially assisted or automated, not a claim that 20% of outsourced BPO operators are fully autonomous. The range remains wide because the available public sources are largely large-provider and platform evidence rather than a 561422 small-bureau census.
narrative.consolidation_economics: There are strategic buyers and large public-service comparables, but their scale is not a read-through to a $1-10M EBITDA domestic bureau. Teleperformance's disclosed Majorel acquisition consideration was about EUR2.659bn and Majorel reported EUR369m 2022 operating EBITDA, implying about 7.2x before differences in debt, scale, geography and synergies [S3]. I use 4.5x as a discounted small-target entry estimate and 3.0x exit because AI-driven seat-volume and pricing risk are likely to widen the gap from that large strategic precedent. That is a judgment, not a disclosed 561422 transaction multiple.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is MELTING, not zero. Some buyers will still pay for regulated, multilingual, sales-sensitive or escalation-heavy human service, and a good operator may sell managed AI operations. But the coded service is operating communication for another party [S1]; when AI handles the common communication, the client can reduce purchased agent capacity or bypass the bureau. The residual human work is likely smaller, harder to staff and less able to support a conventional seat-based revenue base.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central uncertainty is customer and contract mix: a compliance-heavy bureau with outcome-based contracts could retain more value than this score, while a per-FTE campaign shop could deteriorate faster. The deterministic role mix is 4-digit 5614 rather than 561422 and its residual is large, so V is a proxy-based estimate. The multiple evidence is a large international strategic acquisition, not a local acquisition comp. Offshore competition, client concentration, data-security constraints and state/federal telemarketing rules can alter timing without changing the basic separation between labor savings, capture and terminal demand.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 561422 Telemarketing Bureaus and Other Contact Centers
sources.what_it_supports: Exact NAICS scope: third-party contact centers initiate or receive communications for clients and do not own the represented product or service.

sources.title: Concentrix Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: A scaled CX provider identifies AI and self-service as able to replace or supplement human contacts and warns of pricing-model and available-market pressure.

sources.title: Teleperformance amendment to 2022 universal registration document: Majorel acquisition
sources.what_it_supports: Disclosed Majorel acquisition consideration and 2022 operating EBITDA used only as a large, international strategic valuation reference, with an explicit small-target discount.

sources.title: RingCentral 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Contact-center technology vendor disclosure that AI-led products are offered on a usage-based pricing model; this is a platform proxy, not BPO-operator pricing data.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Routine customer-contact, telemarketing, collection, QA and coordination tasks are substantially exposed, while escalations, complex cases and client-specific judgment remain. The 4-digit occupational proxy is imperfect, so the aggregate is bounded rather than presented as observed staffing data.
caveats: The deterministic 4-digit 5614 role mix includes sibling-code occupations and is not a 561422 staffing census.
The 0.4171 residual wage share is an unobserved mix judgment, not a measured 561422 role share.

role_judgments.rationale: Routine service contacts, knowledge retrieval and after-call work are exposed; escalations remain human.
Scripted outbound qualification and reminders are highly automatable, subject to compliance controls.
Routine reminders and documentation automate, while disputes and regulated collection decisions do not fully.
QA analytics, scheduling and coaching support automate; people leadership and escalations remain.
Proposal drafting and research automate, but enterprise selling and RFP relationships remain.
Forecasting and reporting automate partially; client and operating accountability remain.
This is likely proxy contamination; if present, routine clerical interactions are exposed.
This is likely proxy contamination; transcription-like routine work is highly exposed if relevant.
This is likely proxy contamination; mail and routine administrative processing are exposed.
Tier-one support automates but integration and incident work persist.
Unlisted work is assumed to mix routine administrative and client-specific human tasks.

pi_dist.rationale: Third-party delivery, self-service substitution and competitive pricing imply clients retain most efficiency value; differentiated operators retain some execution spread.
caveats: Large-provider and platform disclosures are proxies for small domestic bureau contracts.

pi_moat.rationale: Compliance, integrations and incumbent workflows provide some protection, but the core service is contestable and clients own the product relationship.
caveats: No target-specific client concentration, contract-transfer or certification evidence was available.

t50_years.rationale: Human-contact substitution and usage-priced AI offerings support a near-term transition, moderated by integration, risk review and client adoption.
caveats: This is an effective-work adoption estimate, not a measured 561422 adoption survey.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Existing automation and assisted-agent use justify a nonzero base, but public evidence does not isolate lower-middle-market 561422 operators.
caveats: Do not interpret as the share of companies with fully autonomous contact handling.

historical_analogs.value: IVR, online self-service and offshore delivery show that routine contacts can leave domestic seats; generative AI extends that displacement into conversational and QA tasks.
historical_analogs.rationale: The analogy is directional: prior automation changed channel mix, while current AI also changes task execution.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Faster or slower depending on integration and regulation
caveats: This is a causal analogy, not a measured historical elasticity.

selection.rationale: No code-specific owner-age source was found; use a broad midpoint rather than fabricate precision.
caveats: No observed 561422 ownership-age series was located.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No code-specific documented succession shortage was found in the reopened sources.
caveats: Do not infer a succession shortage from general small-business demographics.

active_consolidators.rationale: Large CX platforms demonstrate strategic activity, but this is an approximate count of plausible national-scale acquirer categories rather than a transaction database count.
caveats: Not a verified count of active buyers for $1-10M EBITDA 561422 targets.

buy_mult.rationale: The EUR2.659bn Majorel consideration and EUR369m operating EBITDA imply about 7.2x for a large international strategic asset; 4.5x discounts sharply for small-target scale, domestic concentration and AI risk.
caveats: The disclosed Majorel precedent is not NAICS 561422, not US lower-middle-market and includes strategic scale/synergy value.

exit_mult.rationale: Exit is below entry because ongoing AI substitution and pricing pressure can erode both volume and buyer underwriting despite large-platform strategic interest.
caveats: There is no disclosed small 561422 exit transaction multiple; this is a risk-adjusted underwriting judgment.

pricing_structure.value: Third-party contact-center delivery commonly monetizes labor or interaction capacity; AI self-service can reduce billable human volume and competitive contracts can pass savings to clients.
pricing_structure.rationale: The code is third-party service delivery, and provider disclosures identify price-model and usage-model change; this supports deflation risk rather than a specific universal contract term.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Seat/hour, interaction, transaction and hybrid contracts
caveats: No target contract sample was reviewed; RingCentral is a technology-vendor proxy rather than an outsourced-BPO operator.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The estimated 48% task share creates meaningful theoretical savings, but a three-year adoption path and low capture mean the value is not a near-term retained-margin thesis.

terminal_value.justification: AI can directly substitute for much of the third-party communication capacity that the code sells, leaving a smaller and more contestable human residual.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No proxy is treated as direct proof: the 4-digit role mix, large-provider filings, technology-vendor pricing and global Majorel transaction are each disclosed as proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing deflation is assigned to capture: fewer human contacts and competitive rebids can reduce billable volume or pass savings to clients, rather than improve terminal demand.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5614 rather than 561422 and includes apparent sibling-code occupations; labor share is a stated cross-vintage dataset input.
sensitivity: A true 561422 wage mix could raise or lower V materially.
W-2 labor share may not represent all delivery cost or client pass-through.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Census NAICS scope and the prompt's lower-middle-market product question; in-house contact centers and sibling telephone-answering services are excluded.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 5.0 to 2.0 across this interval.
Capture changes materially if contracts shift to outcomes rather than pass savings through.
The multiple score is capped at zero at the selected negative-spread case.

reviewer_note: Research packet authored from reopened live sources on 2026-07-21; every score-driving source ID was reopened before finalization.
